title: Question 1.3 (Gowers–Long)
statement_text: Is there any pair $(q,r)$ for which one can prove a power savings $G_{q,r}(n)\leqslant n^{q-r+1-\delta}$, for some absolute constant $\delta>0$?
context_text: Gowers and Long [25] proved numerous results on the extremal functions $F_{q,r}(n)$ and $G_{q,r}(n)$, including a power-savings bound for $F_{q,q-1}(n)$, but their results for $G_{q,r}(n)$ were much more limited: the trivial upper bound $G_{q,r}(n)\leqslant n^{q-r+1}$ remained unimproved in general. This question asks whether any power savings over that trivial bound is achievable. The present paper resolves the question affirmatively for the pair $(q,q-1)$ for every $q$ via Corollary 1.4, by combining the lower bound on $g_{q,q-1}(N)$ from Theorem 1.1 with the known equivalence between the tournament and vector formulations.
paper_contribution: Resolves the question affirmatively: for every $q$ and the pair $(q,q-1)$, the paper's main Theorem 1.1 implies $G_{q,q-1}(n)\leqslant n^{q-(q-1)+1-\delta}$ for an absolute constant $\delta>0$, as stated in Corollary 1.4.

title: Problem 5.1
statement_text: For some integers $q\geqslant 4$ and $N$, does there exist a non-transitive $q$-edge-colored $N$-vertex tournament whose longest color-avoiding path has strictly fewer than $f_{q,q-1}(N)$ vertices?
context_text: For $q\geqslant 4$, Proposition 1.5 shows $F_{q,q-1}(n)=G_{q,q-1}(n)$, meaning the largest $(q-1)$-comparable set is always a $(q-1)$-increasing sequence and hence corresponds to a transitive tournament. Problem 5.1 asks whether a non-transitive coloring can do strictly worse than the transitive extremum. The answer is affirmative for $q=3$ via the Hamaker–Stein construction, but Proposition 1.5 rules out any vector-based construction for $q\geqslant 4$, leaving the problem open in that range.
